blocks.content: Nathan didn't start with the obvious questions. He never did — he came in sideways, through the small things, because he understood that the small things were usually more honest. He started with the arcade, and somehow that led to a full conversation about claw machines that neither of them had planned.

blocks.speaker: Nathan
blocks.content: There's a specific type of person who believes they can beat a claw machine through willpower alone. Like the machine is morally obligated to respond to commitment.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Ethan is that person.

blocks.speaker: Nathan
blocks.content: I know. I've seen it. He gets this look—

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Like the bear personally owes him something.

blocks.speaker: Nathan
blocks.content: Yes. Exactly that look.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: He spent four tokens on the same bear at Galaxy Games. Harold. The bear's name was Harold.

blocks.speaker: Nathan
blocks.content: You named it before you won it?

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: I was manifesting.

blocks.speaker: Nathan
blocks.content: You were catastrophizing and calling it strategy. Did he at least get it?

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Fifth token. He was very dignified about it.

blocks.speaker: Nathan
blocks.content: He was not dignified about it.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Internally dignified. There was a very quiet fist pump.

blocks.speaker: Nathan
blocks.content: That tracks perfectly.
The laughter that moved through the table was easy, and Ethan registered with a detached clarity that he wasn't on the outside of it. He was in it — which was unusual enough to notice.
Nathan let the laughter settle. Then, with the ease of someone who had been moving toward something all along:

blocks.speaker: Nathan
blocks.content: Can I ask you something?

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Go ahead.

blocks.speaker: Nathan
blocks.content: How did you get him to actually talk? Because I've been his friend for six years and there are rooms in him I've seen the doors to but never been inside. I've tried. I've waited. I've given him every possible opening. He's very good at being present without revealing anything.
The table went quiet. The kind of quiet that asks whether someone will answer honestly.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: I didn't get him to do anything. I just didn't give him a way out. I asked questions and then actually waited for the answer. Most people can't sit with silence — they fill it for you, give you an escape route, let you off the hook. If you just let it be there, the other person has to decide whether they're going to step into it or keep hiding. Eventually, most people step in.
She said it simply. Not proud of herself. Just reporting what she'd observed.

blocks.speaker: Nathan
blocks.content: And he stepped in.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: He needed a minute. But yeah.
Nathan was quiet in the way he got when he was recalibrating — not changing his position, but adjusting it.

blocks.speaker: Nathan
blocks.content: That's either very wise or very calculated.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Can't it be both?

blocks.speaker: Nathan
blocks.content: Yeah.
He looked at Ethan then, and in the specific frequency of their friendship — six years of shorthand and understood things — passed something that didn't need words. A recognition. An acknowledgment.
Ethan gave the smallest nod.
